--- SOURCE 1: Seismic Design Of Building Structures - Oahu Chapter ---
URL: https://www.oahu.narpm.org/default.aspx/mL8F86/6041065/Seismic%20Design%20Of%20Building%20Structures.pdf

SUMMARY:
The key principles include ensuring ductility, redundancy, and energy dissipation capacity in the structure, designing for lateral loads induced by earthquakes, and adhering to building codes to prevent collapse and minimize damage during seismic events.

--- SOURCE 2: Seismic Design Principles | WBDG ---
URL: https://www.wbdg.org/resources/seismic-design-principles

SUMMARY:
This resource page provides an introduction to the concepts and principles of seismic design, including strategies for designing earthquake-resistant buildings to ensure the health, safety, and security of building occupants and assets. For example, were the Oklahoma Federal Building designed to seismic design standards, the damage caused by the blast would have been much less (refer to MAT Report FEMA 277). Building configuration determines the way seismic forces are distributed within the structure, their relative magnitude, and problematic design concerns. Knowledge of the building's period, torsion, damping, ductility, strength, stiffness, and configuration can help one determine the most appropriate seismic design devices and mitigation strategies to employ. Base Isolation: This seismic design strategy involves separating the building from the foundation and acts to absorb shock.

FULL CONTENT:
![Home](/sites/default/files/WBDG_logo_hm.png)

## CE Main Menu

# Seismic Design Principles

## Breadcrumb

## Introduction

#### Within This Page

This resource page provides an introduction to the concepts and principles of seismic design, including strategies for designing earthquake-resistant buildings to ensure the [health, safety](/design-objectives/secure-safe/occupant-safety-health), and [security of building occupants and assets](/design-objectives/secure-safe/security-building-occupants-assets).

The essence of successful seismic design is three-fold. First, the design team must take a multi-hazard approach towards design that accounts for the potential impacts of seismic forces as well as all the major hazards to which an area is vulnerable. Second, performance-based requirements, which may exceed the minimum life safety requirements of current seismic codes, must be established to respond appropriately to the threats and risks posed by natural hazards on the building's mission and occupants. Third, and as important as the others, because earthquake forces are dynamic and each building responds according to its own design complexity, it is essential that the design team work collaboratively and have a common understanding of the terms and methods used in the seismic design process.

In addition, as a general rule, buildings designed to resist earthquakes should also [resist blast](/resources/designing-buildings-resist-explosive-threats) (terrorism) or wind, suffering less damage. For example, were the Oklahoma Federal Building designed to seismic design standards, the damage caused by the blast would have been much less (refer to [MAT Report FEMA 277](http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/1967?id=1530)). For more information, see WBDG [Designing Buildings to Resist Explosive Threats](/resources/designing-buildings-resist-explosive-threats) section on Seismic vs. Blast Protection.

## Description

About half of the states and territories in the United States—more than 109 million people and 4.3 million businesses—and most of the other populous regions of the earth are exposed to risks from seismic hazards. In the U.S. alone, the average direct cost of earthquake damage is estimated at $1 billion/year while indirect business losses are estimated to exceed $2 billion/year.

![Seismicity Map of the United States showing the very high areas being on the west coast, Carolinas, and northwestern Tennessee. The high and moderate areas surround these very high areas.](/images/seismic_design_01.jpg)

Seismicity of the United States

### A. Origin and Measurement of Earthquakes

#### Plate Tectonics, the Cause of Earthquakes

Earthquakes are the shaking, rolling, or sudden shock of the earth's surface. Basically, the Earth's crust consists of a series of "plates" floating over the interior, continually moving (at 2 to 130 millimeters per year), spreading from the center, sinking at the edges, and being regenerated. Friction caused by plates colliding, extending, or subducting (one plate slides under the other) builds up stresses that, when released, causes an earthquake to radiate through the crust in a complex wave motion, producing ground failure (in the form of surface faulting [a split in the ground], landslides, liquefaction, or subsidence), or tsunami. This, in turn, can cause anywhere from minor damage to total devastation of the built environment near where the earthquake occurred.

![Photo example of ground failure-landslide - Alaska, 1964](/images/seismic_design_02.jpg)
![Photo example of liquefaction damage-Niigata, Japan 1964](/images/seismic_design_03.jpg)

Ground failure-landslide—Alaska, 1964

Liquefaction damage—Niigata, Japan 1964

![Photo of Saada Hotel before an earthquake destroyed it-Agadir, Morocco, 1960](/images/seismic_design_04.jpg)
![Photo of Saada Hotel after being destroyed by an earthquake-ground shaking damage-Agadir, Morocco, 1960](/images/seismic_design_05.jpg)

Saada Hotel (before)—Agadir, Morocco, 1960

Saada Hotel (after) ground shaking damage—Agadir, Morocco, 1960

#### Measuring Seismic Forces

In order to characterize or measure the effect of an earthquake on the ground (a.k.a. ground motion), the following definitions are commonly used:

If the level of acceleration is combined with duration, the power of destruction is defined. Usually, the longer the duration, the less acceleration the building can endure. A building can withstand very high acceleration for a very short duration in proportion with damping measures incorporated in the structure.

*Intensity* is the amount of damage the earthquake causes locally, which can be characterized by the 12 level *Modified Mercalli Scale* (MM) where each level designates a certain amount of destruction correlated to ground acceleration. Earthquake damage will vary depending on distance from origin (or epicenter), local soil conditions, and the type of construction.

### B. Effects of Earthquakes on Buildings

***Seismic Terminology*** (For definitions of terms used in this resource page, see [Glossary of Seismic Terminology](/files/pdfs/seismic_glossary.pdf) )

The aforementioned seismic measures are used to calculate forces that earthquakes impose on buildings. Ground shaking (pushing back and forth, sideways, up and down) generates internal forces within buildings called the *Inertial Force (FInertial*), which in turn causes most seismic damage.

*FInertial* = Mass (M) X Acceleration (A).

The greater the mass (weight of the building), the greater the internal inertial forces generated. Lightweight construction with less mass is typically an advantage in seismic design. Greater mass generates greater lateral forces, thereby increasing the possibility of columns being displaced, out of plumb, and/or buckling under vertical load (P delta Effect).

Earthquakes generate waves that may be slow and long, or short and abrupt. The length of a full cycle in seconds is the *Period* of the wave and is the inverse of the *Frequency*. All objects, including buildings, have a *natural* or *fundamental period* at which they vibrate if jolted by a shock. The natural period is a primary consideration for seismic design, although other aspects of the building design may also contribute to a lesser degree to the mitigation measures. If the period of the shock wave and the natural period of the building coincide, then the building will "resonate" and its vibration will increase or "amplify" several times.

![Diagram illustrating building height determining Fundamental Period. The diagram shows the equipment at 0.05, 1 story building at 0.1 to 0.5, 4 story building at 1.0 to 2.0, 10-20 story building to 40 story Citicorp at 7.0.](/images/seismic_design_06.gif)

Height is the main determinant of fundamental period—each object has its own fundamental period at which it will vibrate. The period is proportionate to the height of the building.

The soil also has a period varying between 0.4 and 1.5 sec., very soft soil being 2.0 sec. Soft soils generally have a tendency to increase shaking as much as 2 to 6 times as compared to rock. Also, the period of the soil coinciding with the natural period of the building can greatly amplify acceleration of the building and is therefore a design consideration.

![Diagram illustrating modes of building vibration. The first mode shows the building swaying to the right. The second mode shows the building arching to left. The third mode shows the building making a wave.](/images/seismic_design_07.gif)

Tall buildings will undergo several modes of vibration, but for seismic purposes (except for very tall buildings) the fundamental period, or first mode is usually the most significant.

#### Seismic Design Factors

The following factors affect and are affected by the design of the building. It is important that the design team understands these factors and deal with them prudently in the design phase.

*Torsion*: Objects and buildings have a center of mass, a point by which the object (building) can be balanced without rotation occurring. If the mass is uniformly distributed then the geometric center of the floor and the center of mass may coincide. Uneven mass distribution will position the center of mass outside of the geometric center causing "torsion" generating stress concentrations. A certain amount of torsion is unavoidable in every building design. Symmetrical arrangement of masses, however, will result in balanced stiffness against either direction and keep torsion within a manageable range.

*Damping*: Buildings in general are poor resonators to dynamic shock and dissipate vibration by absorbing it. Damping is a rate at which natural vibration is absorbed.

*Ductility*: Ductility is the characteristic of a material (such as steel) to bend, flex, or move, but fails only after considerable deformation has occurred. Non-ductile materials (such as poorly reinforced concrete) fail abruptly by crumbling. Good ductility can be achieved with carefully detailed joints.

*Strength and Stiffness*: Strength is a property of a material to resist and bear applied forces within a safe limit. Stiffness of a material is a degree of resistance to deflection or drift (drift being a horizontal story-to-story relative displacement).

*Building Configuration*: This term defines a building's size and shape, and structural and nonstructural elements. Building configuration determines the way seismic forces are distributed within the structure, their relative magnitude, and problematic design concerns.

![Diagram of irregular and regular building configurations. The irregular configurations are buildings with irregular configuration (T-shaped plan, L-shaped plan, U-shaped plan, Cruciform plan, other complex shapes, multiple towers, split levels, unusually high story, unusually low story, setbacks, and outwardly uniform appearance but non-uniform mass distribution), buildings with abrupt changes in lateral resistance (](/images/seismic_design_08small.gif)
![Photo of collapsed building](/images/seismic_design_09.jpg)

[View enlarged illustration](/images/seismic_design_08large.gif "Irregular and Regular Building Configurations")

Buildings seldom overturn—they fall apart or "pancake"

*Soft First Story* is a discontinuity of strength and stiffness for lateral load at the ground level.

*Discontinuous Shear Walls* do not line up consistently one upon the other causing "soft" levels.

Variation in *Perimeter Strength* and *Stiffness* such as an open front on the ground level usually causes eccentricity or torsion.

Reentrant Corners in the shapes of **H**, **L**, **T**, **U**, **+**, or **[]** develop stress concentration at the reentrant corner and torsion. Seismic designs should adequately separate reentrant corners or strengthen them.

Knowledge of the building's period, torsion, damping, ductility, strength, stiffness, and configuration can help one determine the most appropriate seismic design devices and mitigation strategies to employ.

### C. Seismic Design Strategies and Devices

*Diaphragms*: Floors and roofs can be used as rigid horizontal planes, or diaphragms, to transfer lateral forces to vertical resisting elements such as walls or frames.

*Shear Walls*: Strategically located stiffened walls are shear walls and are capable of transferring lateral forces from floors and roofs to the foundation.

*Braced Frames*: Vertical frames that transfer lateral loads from floors and roofs to foundations. Like shear walls, Braced Frames are designed to take lateral loads but are used where shear walls are impractical.

*Moment-Resistant Frames*: Column/beam joints in moment-resistant frames are designed to take both shear and bending thereby eliminating the space limitations of solid shear walls or braced frames. The column/beam joints are carefully designed to be stiff yet to allow some deformation for energy dissipation taking advantage of the ductility of steel (reinforced concrete can be designed as a Moment-Resistant Frame as well).

![Example of concentric braced frame](/images/seismic_design_10.jpg)
![Example of Eccentric braced frame, with link beams](/images/seismic_design_11.jpg)

Concentric Braced Frame

Eccentric Braced Frame, with link beams

*Energy-Dissipating Devices*: Making the building structure more resistive will increase shaking which may damage the contents or the function of the building. Energy-Dissipating Devices are used to minimize shaking. Energy will dissipate if ductile materials deform in a controlled way. An example is Eccentric Bracing whereby the controlled deformation of framing members dissipates energy. However, this will not eliminate or reduce damage to building contents. A more direct solution is the use of energy dissipating devices that function like shock absorbers in a moving car. The period of the building will be lengthened and the building will "ride out" the shaking within a tolerable range.

![Example of base isolation bearing](/images/seismic_design_12.jpg)

Base Isolation Bearings are used to modify the transmission of the forces from the ground to the building

*Base Isolation*: This seismic design strategy involves separating the building from the foundation and acts to absorb shock. As the ground moves, the building moves at a slower pace because the isolators dissipate a large part of the shock. The building must be designed to act as a unit, or "rigid box", of appropriate height (to avoid overturning) and have flexible utility connections to accommodate movement at its base. Base Isolation is easiest to incorporate in the design of new construction. Existing buildings may require alterations to be made more rigid to move as a unit with foundations separated from the superstructure to insert the Base Isolators. Additional space (a "moat") must be provided for horizontal displacement (the whole building will move back and forth a whole foot or more). Base Isolation retrofit is a costly operation that is most commonly appropriate in high asset value facilities and may require partial or the full removal of building occupants during installation.

![Photo example of fluid viscous dampers](/images/seismic_design_13.jpg)

Passive Energy Dissipation includes the introduction of devices such as dampers to dissipate earthquake energy producing friction or deformation.

The materials used for *Elastomeric Isolators* are natural rubber, high-damping rubber, or another elastomer in combination with metal parts. *Frictive Isolators* are also used and are made primarily of metal parts.

Tall buildings cannot be base-isolated or they would overturn. Being very flexible compared to low-rise buildings, their horizontal displacement needs to be controlled. This can be achieved by the use of *Dampers*, which absorb a good part of the energy making the displacement tolerable. Retrofitting existing buildings is often easier with dampers than with base isolators, especially if the application is external or does not interfere with the occupants.

There are many types of dampers used to mitigate seismic effects, including:

### D. Nonstructural Damage Control

All items, which are not part of the structural system, are considered as "nonstructural", and include such building elements as:

These items must be stabilized with bracing to prevent their damage or total destruction. Building machinery and equipment can be outfitted with seismic isolating devices, which are modified versions of the standard Vibration Isolators.

Loss arising from nonstructural damage can be a multiple of the structural losses. Loss of business and failure of entire businesses was very high in the Loma Prieta, Northridge, and Kobe earthquakes due to both structural and nonstructural seismic damages.

## Application

The principles and strategies of seismic design and construction are applied in a systematic approach that matches an appropriate response to specific conditions through the following major steps:

### 1. Analyze Site Conditions

The location and physical properties of the [site](/design-objectives/sustainable/optimize-site-potential) are the primary influences the entire design process. The following questions can serve as a checklist to identify seismic design objectives.

Consider mission critical or business continuity threats of seismicity on adjacent sites or elsewhere in the vicinity that may render the project site inaccessible or causes the loss of utilities, threat of fire, or the release of toxic materials to the site. Conduct subsurface investigations to discover loose soils or uncontrolled fill that could increase ground motion. Hard dense soils remain more stable, while solid dense rock is the most predictable and seismically safe building base.

### 2. Establish Seismic Design Objectives

A performance-based approach to establishing seismic [design objectives](/design-objectives/functional-operational/meet-performance-objectives) is recommended. This determines a level of predictable building behavior by responding to the maximum considered earthquake. A [threat/vulnerability assessment and risk analysis](/resources/threat-vulnerability-assessments-and-risk-analysis) can be used to define the level of performance desired for the building project. Some suggested seismic design performance goals are:

Regarding the magnitude of the earthquake it may also be stipulated as "Low," "Moderate," or "Large" as another matrix of grading threat and establishing corresponding building performance goals.

### 3. Select/Design Appropriate Structural Systems

Seismic design objectives can greatly influence the selection of the most appropriate structural system and related building systems for the project. Some construction type options, and corresponding seismic properties, are:

Structural and architectural detailing and construction quality control is very important to ensure ductility and natural damping and to keep damages to a limited and repairable range. The prospect of structural and nonstructural damage is not likely to be eliminated without the prudent use of energy-dissipating devices. The cost of adding energy-dissipating devices is in the range of 1–2% of the total structural cost. This is not a large number, particularly when related to the [life-cycle cost](/resources/life-cycle-cost-analysis-lcca) of the building. Within a 30–50 year life cycle the cost is negligible.

## Relevant Codes and Standards

Many building codes and governmental standards exist pertaining to design and construction for seismic hazard mitigation. As previously mentioned, building code requirements are primarily prescriptive and define seismic zones and minimum safety factors to "design to." Codes pertaining to seismic requirements may be local, state, or regional building codes or amendments and should be researched thoroughly by the design professional.

Many governmental agencies at the federal level have seismic standards, criteria, and program specialists who are involved in major building programs and can give further guidance on special requirements.

## Additional Resources

For definitions of terms used in this resource page, see [Glossary of Seismic Terminology](/files/pdfs/seismic_glossary.pdf) .

Seismic Design Principles

Seismic Design Principles

Understanding Seismic Design Principles
=======================================

**What are Seismic Design Principles?**

 Seismic design principles are guidelines used to create buildings and structures that can withstand earthquakes. These principles help engineers make structures safer by ensuring they can handle the shaking from seismic activity.

Why Seismic Design Matters
--------------------------

Earthquakes can be very dangerous. When they happen, buildings that are not designed properly can collapse, causing injury and damage. By following seismic design principles, engineers can minimize risk and protect lives.

Key Elements of Seismic Design Principles
-----------------------------------------

1.   **Building Shape:** The shape of a building can affect how it moves during an earthquake. Simple shapes are usually better because they can move more evenly.

2.   **Materials Used:** Strong materials help buildings stay upright when they shake. Steel and reinforced concrete are common choices in seismic design.

3.   **Foundation Design:** A strong foundation is crucial for any building. It keeps the structure stable during seismic events. Deep foundations are often used in earthquake-prone areas.

4.   **Damping Systems:** These systems absorb some of the energy from an earthquake. They help buildings sway less, reducing the risk of damage.

5.   **Regular Inspections:** Buildings should be inspected regularly to make sure they follow seismic design principles. This helps identify any weaknesses.

Why Assess a Candidate’s Seismic Design Principles?
---------------------------------------------------

Assessing a candidate’s seismic design principles is important for several reasons:

1.   **Safety Matters:** Buildings need to be safe, especially in areas where earthquakes are common. A strong understanding of seismic design helps ensure that structures can withstand shaking and protect people's lives.

2.   **Quality Engineering:** Candidates with knowledge of seismic design principles show that they can use the best practices in engineering. This leads to higher quality buildings that last longer and require less maintenance over time.

3.   **Regulatory Compliance:** Many places have strict building codes related to earthquakes. Assessing a candidate’s knowledge ensures that your projects will meet these codes, avoiding legal issues and costly fines.

4.   **Cost Efficiency:** Well-designed buildings can reduce repair costs after an earthquake. Hiring someone who understands these principles can save money in the long run by preventing damage.

5.   **Innovation in Design:** Candidates skilled in seismic design principles can bring new ideas to your projects. This can lead to unique and effective solutions that help your buildings stand out.

By assessing a candidate’s seismic design principles, you are helping to ensure that your projects are safe, compliant, and of high quality. This not only protects people but also enhances your company’s reputation in the industry.

How to Assess Candidates on Seismic Design Principles
-----------------------------------------------------

Assessing candidates on their knowledge of seismic design principles is essential for ensuring that your team is equipped to handle earthquake-resistant construction. Here are a couple of effective ways to evaluate candidates for their skills in this area:

### 1. Knowledge Assessments

Using knowledge assessments is a practical way to test a candidate's understanding of seismic design principles. These assessments can include multiple-choice questions, true/false questions, or scenario-based questions that require problem-solving. By asking about key topics like building codes, foundation design, and materials, you can determine how well a candidate grasps the fundamental concepts.

### 2. Practical Work Simulations

Practical work simulations allow candidates to demonstrate their skills in real-world scenarios. For example, you can present a case study of a building in an earthquake-prone area and ask candidates to propose seismic design solutions. This hands-on approach helps you evaluate not only their knowledge but also their ability to apply that knowledge effectively.

By utilizing tools like Alooba, you can streamline the assessment process. Alooba provides a platform for creating and administering these tests efficiently. This ensures that you can quickly identify the best candidates with the right skills in seismic design principles, ultimately leading to safer and more resilient structures.

Topics and Subtopics in Seismic Design Principles
-------------------------------------------------

Understanding seismic design principles involves various key topics and subtopics that help engineers create safe and effective buildings. Here are the main areas of focus:

### 1. **Fundamentals of Seismic Design**

*   Definition of Seismic Design Principles
*   Importance of Earthquake-Resistant Structures
*   Overview of Earthquake Forces

### 2. **Seismic Analysis Methods**

*   Static Analysis
*   Dynamic Analysis
*   Response Spectrum Analysis
*   Time History Analysis

### 3. **Design Criteria and Building Codes**

*   Overview of Local and International Building Codes
*   Seismic Design Categories
*   Importance of Compliance with Regulations

### 4. **Structural Systems and Materials**

*   Types of Structural Systems (Moment-Resisting Frames, Shear Walls, etc.)
*   Material Properties (Reinforced Concrete, Steel, etc.)
*   Damping Systems and Energy Dissipation

### 5. **Site Considerations**

*   Soil Mechanism and Its Impact on Seismic Performance
*   Ground Motion Characteristics
*   Site-Specific Seismic Hazard Assessment

### 6. **Foundation Design**

*   Types of Foundations (Shallow vs. Deep Foundations)
*   Foundation Systems for Earthquake Resistance
*   Bearing Capacity and Settlement Issues

### 7. **Connections and Detailing**

*   Importance of Proper Connections in Seismic Design
*   Design of Structural Joints
*   Rules for Reinforcement Detailing

### 8. **Retrofitting and Rehabilitation**

*   Methods for Improving Existing Structures
*   Assessment of Vulnerable Structures
*   Costs and Benefits of Retrofitting

By covering these topics and subtopics, engineers gain a comprehensive understanding of seismic design principles. This knowledge is crucial for designing buildings that can withstand seismic forces, ensuring safety and durability in earthquake-prone areas.

How Seismic Design Principles Are Used
--------------------------------------

Seismic design principles are essential in the field of civil engineering, particularly for buildings and structures in earthquake-prone areas. Here’s how these principles are applied in practice:

### 1. **Designing New Structures**

When engineers design new buildings, they incorporate seismic design principles to ensure safety and stability during earthquakes. This involves analyzing potential seismic forces and selecting appropriate materials and structural systems. By following these guidelines, engineers can create structures that can resist shaking and minimize damage.

### 2. **Assessing Existing Buildings**

Seismic design principles are also used to evaluate the safety of existing buildings. Engineers assess vulnerable structures to determine their ability to withstand earthquakes. If weaknesses are found, retrofitting measures can be recommended to enhance resilience, ensuring the safety of occupants and minimizing property damage.

### 3. **Developing Building Codes**

Local and international building codes include seismic design principles to set standards for construction in earthquake-prone regions. These codes provide guidelines for engineers and architects, promoting best practices in seismic design. Compliance with these codes is crucial for obtaining building permits and ensuring community safety.

### 4. **Educating Engineers and Architects**

Seismic design principles are a key part of civil engineering and architecture education. By teaching future professionals about these principles, colleges and universities ensure that new engineers are prepared to design safe structures that can withstand earthquakes.

### 5. **Conducting Research and Innovations**

Ongoing research in seismic design principles helps improve existing methods and develop new technologies. Innovations in materials, design strategies, and analysis techniques continue to enhance the effectiveness of seismic design, leading to safer buildings and communities.

In summary, the application of seismic design principles is crucial for creating resilient structures, assessing existing buildings, developing building codes, educating future professionals, and promoting research and innovation in the field. By prioritizing seismic design, communities can better protect lives and property from the impacts of earthquakes.

Roles That Require Good Seismic Design Principles Skills
--------------------------------------------------------

Several roles in the construction and engineering industries benefit significantly from strong knowledge of seismic design principles. Here are some key positions:

### 1. **Structural Engineer**

Structural engineers are responsible for designing buildings and structures that can withstand various forces, including earthquakes. Their expertise in seismic design principles helps them create safe and durable solutions. To learn more about this role, visit Structural Engineer.

### 2. **Civil Engineer**

Civil engineers work on a wide range of projects, including roads, bridges, and buildings. Having a solid understanding of seismic design principles is vital for ensuring the safety and longevity of these structures, especially in earthquake-prone areas. Explore more about this role at Civil Engineer.

### 3. **Architect**

Architects play a crucial role in planning and designing buildings. A good grasp of seismic design principles is essential for architects to ensure that their designs meet safety standards and can withstand potential earthquakes. Find out more about the requirements for this role at Architect.

### 4. **Geotechnical Engineer**

Geotechnical engineers assess soil and site conditions to inform building design. Their work is crucial for understanding how ground conditions affect seismic performance, making knowledge of seismic design principles essential. Learn more about this role at Geotechnical Engineer.

### 5. **Building Inspector**

Building inspectors must verify that structures comply with local building codes, including those related to seismic safety. A strong understanding of seismic design principles is essential for accurately evaluating whether buildings are safe and up to standards. More details about this role can be found at Building Inspector.

By possessing good seismic design principles skills, professionals in these roles can contribute to creating safer, more resilient structures that protect lives and property in the event of an earthquake.

Associated Roles
----------------

### Civil Engineer

A Civil Engineer is a professional responsible for designing, constructing, and maintaining infrastructure projects, ensuring compliance with local building codes and international standards. They apply their expertise in structural analysis, foundation design, and geotechnical engineering to create safe and sustainable structures.

[View Details](https://www.alooba.com/roles/civil-engineer/)

# Basic Principles of Seismic Design for Buildings

![Basic Principles of Seismic Design for Buildings](https://www.ny-engineers.com/hs-fs/hubfs/seismicdesign.jpg?width=1750&height=276&name=seismicdesign.jpg "Basic Principles of Seismic Design for Buildings")

About half of the US territory is exposed to risks from seismic hazards. The average direct cost of earthquake damage is approximately $1 billion per year, and indirect costs can go up to $2 billion per year. The construction industry is constantly looking for effective ways to [protect buildings from natural hazards](/blog/hurricane-safety-for-construction-sites), and there are several approaches to seismic design.

This article discusses some basic principles of seismic design, where the main goal is ensuring safety for occupants. There are three important requirements before starting a seismic design:

## Design your building to be resilient during emergencies.

[![CONTACT US](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/2004318/2c2fda64-a5d2-4d92-b73c-1db5d7a5d391.png)](https://cta-redirect.hubspot.com/cta/redirect/2004318/2c2fda64-a5d2-4d92-b73c-1db5d7a5d391)

![CONTACT US](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/2004318/2c2fda64-a5d2-4d92-b73c-1db5d7a5d391.png)

## Defining an Earthquake

An earthquake is a sudden shaking of the Earth’s surface. The planetary crust consists of a series of plates that are in constant movement, and stress builds as the plates collide, extend and subduct. When this stress is released, it radiates through the crust producing earthquakes and tsunamis. Depending on where their occur, these natural disasters have the potential to cause severe damage.

## How Are Seismic Forces Measured?

There are several key terms used to describe and measure the effects of an earthquake on the ground:

The destructive power of an earthquake is determined by the acceleration and duration. Generally, the longer an earthquake lasts, the less acceleration a building can withstand.  

## Inertial Force

The seismic measures mentioned before are used to calculate the forces that affect buildings. The movements caused by earthquakes generate internal forces within buildings, which cause most of the structural damage.

The **inertial force** is the result of multiplying the weight of a building and the acceleration of an earthquake. The greater the mass of a building, the greater the inertial force. Lightweight constructions have an advantage in seismic design, while heavier buildings will generate greater forces, which increases the possibility of column failure.

![earthquake damage](https://www.ny-engineers.com/hs-fs/hubfs/earthquakedamage.jpg?width=602&name=earthquakedamage.jpg)

![earthquake damage](https://www.ny-engineers.com/hs-fs/hubfs/earthquakedamage.jpg?width=602&name=earthquakedamage.jpg)

## Fundamental Period

Earthquakes generate waves that can be long and slow or short and abrupt. The period of the wave is the length of a full cycle in seconds, the frequency is the number of cycles per second. 

The natural frequency of a building is a primary consideration for seismic design, but there are other aspects that mitigate the impact of an earthquake to a lesser degree.

Height is one of the main factors that determines the natural frequency of a building. The oscillation period is proportional to the height of a building, meaning that taller buildings will have a lower natural frequency. [Tall buildings](/blog/making-high-rise-buildings-more-resilient-under-extreme-weather) undergo several modes of vibration, but the fundamental period or first mode is usually the most significant for seismic purposes. Note that other modes of vibration may be used for analysis with very tall buildings.

Soil is another important factor to consider during seismic design. Typically, soil has a period ranging from 0.4 to 1.5 seconds, and soft soils have shorter periods. Soft soils tend to increase shaking by 2 to 6 times compared with rock. It is important to consider that the period of the soil can coincide with the fundamental period of the building, which will amplify the acceleration of the building.

![Keith Fink](https://www.ny-engineers.com/hs-fs/hubfs/New-Images/Keith-Cut.png?width=150&name=Keith-Cut.png "Keith Fink")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Ductility refers to a material's ability to deform without breaking, allowing structures to absorb and dissipate energy during seismic events, thus preventing abrupt failures.
Energy dissipation involves mechanisms that absorb seismic energy, such as dampers, reducing the energy transferred to the structure and minimizing potential damage.
Load path continuity ensures that seismic forces are effectively transmitted throughout the structure, maintaining stability and preventing localized failures that could lead to collapse.
Base isolation involves decoupling a building from ground motion, using flexible bearings that allow movement and absorb shock, significantly enhancing seismic resilience.
These principles are critical for designing earthquake-resistant structures, ensuring occupant safety, minimizing damage, and enhancing overall building performance during seismic events.
